title: Aurum a typical MLM or Ponzi scam or legit ?
author: Vikingmelon
selftext:
A friend brought myself and another mutual friend to an event in london with a few interesting characters pushing hard promotion on the amazing returns and the incredible proprietary edge AI chatbots with monthly returns of 12-18 percent. 
There's an option to build a network and get returns off new deposits down your network in true MLM fashion. 
Definitely a slick presentation but ask chat gpt for a summary and it just sums it up as a classic Scam. 
With too goo to be true profits paid out to users from new deposits on these networks bring built instead of real returns.
Anyone have an informed opinion?

comments.author: Ok_Refrigerator2078
comments.body: It has all the hallmarks of an MLM ponzi. Zero proof they are actually trading anything. So many red flags. I’d be shocked if it didn’t all collapse in the near future - basically when fresh incoming money dries up.

comments.author: No_East_5225
comments.body:
# Warning: Major Red Flags with “Aurum” (Do Your Homework)

I dug into this and here are the biggest issues—straight facts, no hype.

# 1) The Returns Don’t Pass Basic Math

They claim:

* \~1% per day
* \~16% per month
* “no losing days/months”

That implies extreme annual returns. If that were real:

* they wouldn’t need retail money
* they wouldn’t rely on referrals
* they wouldn’t be pitching it over Zoom

Consistent high returns with no losses is not how real trading works.

# 2) You Make Money From People, Not Just “The Bot”

Their own materials show:

* referral links
* “legacy levels”
* income from team deposits

Your earnings depend on:

* recruiting people
* how much they deposit
* how long funds stay in the system

That’s a multi-level payout structure, not pure investment performance.

# 3) The “Canadian License” Is Misleading

They claim a “full Canadian license.”

What actually exists:

* a basic business registration in British Columbia

When you check the official Canadian regulator database (CSA), Aurum is not listed as a licensed investment firm.

Registering a company is not the same as being authorized to manage money.

# 4) Timeline Doesn’t Add Up

They claim:

* operating since 2023
* consistent results

But:

* their main domain (aurum.foundation) was only registered in August 2024
* access from the U.S. often requires a VPN

So where was the system operating before that? And why block direct access from the U.S.?

Legit firms have a continuous, verifiable footprint and don’t require workarounds just to view the site.

# 5) No Independent Proof of Trading

Everything shown is:

* dashboards
* screenshots
* internal “trade logs”

None of it is:

* verifiable on a real exchange
* tied to order IDs
* independently audited

If the trading is real, it should be easy to verify externally.

# Bottom Line

This setup shows multiple known risk patterns:

* unrealistic returns
* recruitment-based income
* misleading regulatory claims
* inconsistent timeline and restricted access
* no verifiable trading

Do your due diligence before putting money—or your reputation—into something like this.

replies.author: Busy-Marionberry-588
replies.body: It is a scam, stay always. A couple of weeks ago I lost money. Account has been hackered, no any explanation from Aurum. The event happened the same day a phishing alert was on. They stolen my entire account. Stay away from it

comments.author: Obvious_Sherbet_2981
comments.body: I did some research and created this educaion dilocument.   Stay away.  https://aurum-fraud-report-3h0oqys99-agis-engs-projects.vercel.app/

replies.author: Vikingmelon
replies.body: Thanks! Very comprehensive report. Its clearly dodgy as hell
